title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 11
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (composite, late Vedic prose)
summary: The eleventh kāṇḍa (book) of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, the great prose commentary on the rituals of the White (Śukla) Yajurveda. This book treats the full or new-moon sacrifices (darśapūrṇamāsa), the establishment of sacred fires (agnyādheya), upanayana (initiation), and includes celebrated cosmogonic and ritual-symbolic narratives. It expounds the meaning, mythology, and procedure behind the sacrificial acts rather than merely prescribing them.
history: The Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa ('Brāhmaṇa of a hundred paths') survives in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions and is traditionally associated with Yājñavalkya. Its prose is among the most extensive of the Vedic Brāhmaṇa literature and is a foundational source for early Indian ritual, cosmology, and proto-philosophical speculation that culminates in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 13
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (composite, late Vedic prose)
summary: The thirteenth kāṇḍa of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, dealing chiefly with the great royal and grand sacrifices: the aśvamedha (horse sacrifice), the puruṣamedha, the sarvamedha, and the pitṛmedha (rites for the ancestors/dead). It gives the procedure and symbolic interpretation of these sovereign and cosmic rituals.
history: Part of the most extensive of the Vedic Brāhmaṇas, transmitted in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions of the White Yajurveda and traditionally linked to Yājñavalkya. Its account of the aśvamedha is a key source for the ideology of Vedic kingship.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa 14
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the school of Yājñavalkya)
genre: brahmana / aranyaka / upanishad (Vedic ritual and esoteric exegesis)
date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (composite, late Vedic prose)
summary: The fourteenth and final kāṇḍa of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. Its first part expounds the pravargya rite and forms the Āraṇyaka portion, while its concluding adhyāyas constitute the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, one of the oldest and most important Upaniṣads. Thus this book moves from ritual into the foundational metaphysics of brahman and ātman.
history: Because the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad is embedded here, this book is pivotal in the transition from Vedic ritualism to Upaniṣadic philosophy. Transmitted in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions and traditionally connected with the sage Yājñavalkya, who is the central figure of the embedded Upaniṣad.

title: Śāṅkhāyana-Āraṇyaka
author: anonymous/traditional (Śāṅkhāyana/Kauṣītaki school)
summary: The Āraṇyaka of the Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki) school of the Rigveda, comprising fifteen chapters (adhyāyas). It treats the mahāvrata rite, prāṇa (vital-breath) speculation, the symbolism of the uktha, and esoteric ritual doctrine; its central chapters constitute the Kauṣītaki Upaniṣad and its seventh and eighth chapters form the Saṃhitopaniṣad. It bridges ritual practice and meditative-philosophical reflection.
history: Closely parallel to the Aitareya Āraṇyaka of the same Veda, it belongs to the Kauṣītaki/Śāṅkhāyana branch and is a primary witness to Rigvedic Upaniṣadic thought. Edited and translated into English by A. B. Keith.

title: Āgamaśāstra 1: Āgamaprakaraṇa
author: Gauḍapāda
summary: The first chapter (Āgama-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on Tradition', 29 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra, also called the Māṇḍūkya-kārikā or Gauḍapādīya-kārikā. Interwoven with the twelve mantras of the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad, it expounds the four states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep, and turīya) and the syllable OM as the symbol of ātman/brahman, grounding non-dualism in scriptural tradition.
history: Gauḍapāda is traditionally regarded as the parama-guru (teacher's teacher) of Śaṅkara and the earliest systematic exponent of Advaita Vedānta. Bhattacharya argued the four prakaraṇas were originally independent treatises later consolidated under the single title Āgamaśāstra; the fourth shows marked Mahāyāna Buddhist influence.

title: Āgamaśāstra 2: Vaitathyaprakaraṇa
author: Gauḍapāda
summary: The second chapter (Vaitathya-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on Unreality/Illusion', 38 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra. Using the analogy of the dream, it argues that the objects of waking experience are as unreal (vaitathya) as dream objects, since both are mental projections, thereby establishing the illusoriness of the phenomenal world.
history: One of the four treatises that make up Gauḍapāda's foundational Advaita work. Its dream argument and analysis of unreality parallel and engage Mahāyāna Buddhist (especially Yogācāra/Madhyamaka) reasoning, a much-debated feature of Gauḍapāda's thought.

title: Āgamaśāstra 3: Advaitaprakaraṇa
author: Gauḍapāda
summary: The third chapter (Advaita-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on Non-duality', 48 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra. It establishes the doctrine of non-origination (ajātivāda)—that nothing is ever truly born or destroyed—and presents the absolute non-dual reality of brahman, illustrated by the image of space enclosed in pots.
history: Considered the philosophical heart of Gauḍapāda's work, where ajātivāda is most fully developed. This non-origination doctrine became central to later Advaita and shows Gauḍapāda's distinctive synthesis of Upaniṣadic and Buddhist dialectical methods.

title: Āgamaśāstra 4: Alātaśāntiprakaraṇa
author: Gauḍapāda
tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta), with strong Mahāyāna Buddhist coloring
summary: The fourth and longest chapter (Alātaśānti-prakaraṇa, 'Treatise on the Quenching of the Firebrand', 100 verses) of Gauḍapāda's Āgamaśāstra. Its title refers to the circle of fire seen when a firebrand (alāta) is whirled—an illusion with no real circle—used to argue that the appearances of consciousness have no real arising. It develops non-origination through dialectical arguments markedly close to Buddhist thought.
history: This chapter notably opens with what reads as homage to the Buddha and deploys Madhyamaka- and Yogācāra-style reasoning, making it the focus of scholarly debate over Buddhist influence on early Advaita. Some scholars have even questioned whether it shares authorship with the first three chapters.

title: Aitareya-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the seer Aitareya Mahidāsa)
date_estimate: c. 6th-5th c. BCE (pre-Buddhist, one of the older Upaniṣads)
summary: One of the principal (mukhya) Upaniṣads, embedded in the Aitareya Āraṇyaka of the Rigveda. It describes the creation of the cosmos and of the human person by the ātman/brahman, the entry of the Self into the body, the three births of a person, and culminates in the mahāvākya 'prajñānam brahma' (consciousness is brahman).
history: Traditionally ascribed to the sage Mahidāsa Aitareya and counted among the oldest Upaniṣads. It was commented upon by Śaṅkara and other Vedāntic ācāryas and is a standard source-text for the doctrine of brahman as consciousness.

title: Aitareya-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to the seer Aitareya Mahidāsa)
date_estimate: c. 6th-5th c. BCE (text); commentary c. 8th c. CE
summary: A second encoded version of the Aitareya Upaniṣad, this work_id (suffix 'sb') likely representing the text as accompanied by Śaṅkara's Bhāṣya. Content is identical to the principal Upaniṣad: cosmic and personal creation from the Self, the three births, and the realization 'prajñānam brahma'.
history: Duplicate/recensional entry of the Aitareya Upaniṣad; the variant id signals a distinct edition or the version embedded in a commentary (likely Śaṅkara's). See the parallel entry GV05aitup__u.

title: Brahmabindu-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A short minor Upaniṣad (also called Amṛtabindu Upaniṣad), classed among the Yoga Upaniṣads and attached to the Krishna Yajurveda. In about twenty-two verses it teaches that the mind is the cause of bondage and liberation, distinguishes pure ('śuddha') from impure mind, and advocates meditation on OM and on the silent, attributeless brahman beyond sound.
history: One of the corpus of minor Upaniṣads later than the principal Upaniṣads, transmitted within the canon of (commonly) 108 Upaniṣads. Its precise date is unknown; its yogic and nirguṇa-brahman emphasis reflect post-classical Vedānta-Yoga synthesis.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (closely associated with the sage Yājñavalkya)
date_estimate: c. 7th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest Upaniṣads)
summary: The 'Great Forest Teaching', the largest and one of the very oldest of the principal Upaniṣads, forming the concluding part of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa (book 14). It contains the famous dialogues of Yājñavalkya (with King Janaka, with Gārgī, and with his wife Maitreyī), the identification of ātman with brahman, the doctrine 'neti neti', the transmigration of the self, and the prayer 'lead me from the unreal to the real'.
history: A foundational text of Vedānta, repeatedly cited by Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, Madhva and others, and central to the philosophy of the Self. It survives in the Mādhyandina and Kāṇva recensions; this entry represents the whole Upaniṣad (the per-adhyāya splits follow below).

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
tradition: Hindu (Vedic / Vedānta; White Yajurveda, Kāṇva recension)
date_estimate: c. 7th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest Upaniṣads)
summary: The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad as transmitted in/with the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa context (the 'sb' suffix), i.e. the full Upaniṣad embedded in book 14 of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. Content matches the principal Upaniṣad: the Yājñavalkya dialogues, ātman-brahman identity, 'neti neti', and the doctrine of the self and rebirth.
history: A recensional/encoding variant of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka highlighting its embedding in the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa. The Kāṇva recension is the most widely commented form. See companion per-adhyāya entries.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 2
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
summary: The second chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (Śatapatha recension). It includes the dialogue of the proud Gārgya Bālāki with King Ajātaśatru on the nature of brahman, the teaching of the self as the honey (madhu) of all beings, and the first dialogue of Yājñavalkya with his wife Maitreyī on immortality and the love of the Self.
history: One of three thematic divisions (the 'Madhu-kāṇḍa') of the Upaniṣad. Heavily commented on by Śaṅkara and subsequent Vedāntins. This is a sectional unit of the larger Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 3
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
summary: The third chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad. Set at King Janaka's sacrificial assembly, it presents the famous contest in which Yājñavalkya answers a series of brahmin questioners—including Ārtabhāga, Uṣasta, Kahola, and especially the woman philosopher Gārgī Vācaknavī—on death, the imperishable (akṣara), and the ground of reality.
history: Part of the 'Yājñavalkya-kāṇḍa', this chapter is celebrated for its dialectical philosophical debate and for Gārgī as an early female interlocutor. A central source for the apophatic theology of brahman.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 4
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
summary: The fourth chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad. It continues Yājñavalkya's instruction of King Janaka on the nature of the self, the states of waking, dream, and deep sleep, the self as 'self-luminous' light, the process of dying and rebirth, and the famous 'neti neti' (not this, not this) characterization of brahman; it closes with the second Maitreyī dialogue.
history: Belongs to the 'Yājñavalkya-kāṇḍa'. Its analysis of dream and dreamless sleep and of the transmigrating self deeply shaped later Vedāntic psychology and was extensively interpreted by Śaṅkara.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 5
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
summary: The fifth chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, opening the 'Khila-kāṇḍa' (supplementary book). It comprises a series of shorter teachings and meditations, including the meaning of the syllable OM and the fullness ('pūrṇam adaḥ pūrṇam idam'), the meditation on brahman as the heart, and the celebrated 'da-da-da' parable of Prajāpati teaching self-control (damyata), giving (datta), and compassion (dayadhvam).
history: A supplementary, more liturgical and meditative division of the Upaniṣad. The 'da-da-da' passage became famous worldwide through T. S. Eliot's use of it in 'The Waste Land'.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad: Adhyāya 6
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya)
summary: The sixth and final chapter of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (concluding the Khila-kāṇḍa). It treats the supremacy of the vital breath (prāṇa) among the faculties, the doctrine of the five fires and the two paths of the dead (devayāna and pitṛyāna), rites for conception and the begetting of a learned son, and ends with the lineage (vaṃśa) of teachers transmitting the doctrine.
history: Contains the early 'doctrine of the five fires' and rebirth (pañcāgni-vidyā) shared with the Chāndogya Upaniṣad, an important source for theories of transmigration. The closing teacher-lineage documents the chain of oral transmission.

title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad (Madhvabhāṣya)
author: Madhva (Madhvācārya / Ānandatīrtha)
summary: Madhvācārya's commentary (bhāṣya) on the Chāndogya Upaniṣad, interpreting this major Sāmavedic Upaniṣad through the lens of Dvaita (dualist) Vedānta. Madhva reads its teachings—including the mahāvākya 'tat tvam asi' and the Sat-Vidyā—as affirming an eternal, real distinction between the supreme Lord (Viṣṇu) and the individual soul, against the Advaitic non-dual reading.
history: Madhva, the 13th-century founder of the Dvaita school, wrote bhāṣyas on the principal Upaniṣads to support his realist, theistic Vedānta; this one was further elaborated by sub-commentators such as Vyāsatīrtha (the Ṭippaṇī). It is a key polemical and exegetical text of the Mādhva tradition.

title: Garbha-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: A short minor Upaniṣad attached to the Krishna Yajurveda, distinctive for its embryological and physiological content. It describes the formation and development of the embryo (garbha) month by month in the womb, the composition of the body from the elements and humors, the entry of consciousness into the foetus, and reflections on suffering, rebirth, and liberation that prompt detachment.
history: Counted among the minor Upaniṣads (and grouped with the few 'physiological' Upaniṣads). It blends Vedāntic and early Āyurvedic-style anatomical and embryological ideas, making it a notable source for ancient Indian conceptions of conception and human development; its precise date is unknown.
